title: Making a plying ball
subtitle: Winding singles from one cop into a centre-pull ball for two-ply spinning on a drop spindle
excerpt: A plying ball lets you ply a two-ply yarn on a drop spindle using both ends of a single cop of singles, without a lazy kate or a second bobbin. This entry covers winding an Andean plying bracelet and a centre-pull ball and shows how to ply evenly from both ends.
sourceNotes: The Andean plying bracelet method is a traditional Andean spinning technique widely taught in British spinning guilds as a no-equipment plying method for drop spindle spinning.

glossaryTerms.definition: The unplied yarn produced directly from the fibre supply on the spindle. Singles carry active twist; plying in the opposite direction balances the twist and produces a stable finished yarn.

A yarn made from two singles twisted together in the opposite direction to the singles twist. A two-ply from one cop of singles produces the same amount of finished yarn as the original singles, at roughly half the original length.
A method of winding a cop of singles onto the hand in a specific figure-of-eight pattern so that both ends emerge at the palm, allowing two-ply spinning without a second bobbin.

content.text: When spinning on a drop spindle you often have only one cop of
method winds the singles onto your hand so both ends are accessible at once, letting you make a
on the same spindle. The result is a balanced plied yarn from a single cop, with no equipment other than the spindle and your hand.

Hold the free end of the singles between your little finger and palm. Wind the yarn over the thumb, across the back of the hand, and over the little finger in a figure-of-eight pattern. Continue winding evenly around thumb and little finger until all the singles are wound. Slide the bracelet off the hand as a complete ball. Both the original tail end (from the little finger) and the outside end (from the top of the bracelet) are now accessible.

Take both the inner tail end and the outer end of the bracelet and tie them together to a new leader on the spindle. Spin the spindle counterclockwise (opposite to the Z-twist of the singles) and allow both ends to ply together as you let out yarn from the bracelet. Keep the bracelet in a bowl on the floor so it unwinds smoothly. Check twist balance periodically by letting a loop of the plied yarn hang free; it should hang straight. Wind off onto the niddy-noddy.

items.problem: The bracelet tangles as it unwinds
items.cause: The winding pattern was inconsistent, causing the inner and outer ends to cross
items.fix: Wind more slowly and keep each circuit of the figure-of-eight clearly separate from the last. Place the bracelet in a bowl or small box as you ply so it can rotate freely rather than dragging on the floor.

items.problem: The plied yarn is unbalanced and kinks back on itself
items.cause: Too much plying twist was added
items.fix: Ply with fewer treadles or spindle spins per inch. Check the balance frequently: a well-balanced two-ply from this method hangs straight when a 30 cm loop is doubled and allowed to dangle from one end.
